data.title: Chevron: Provides Appealing Growth Outlook, Entrance Into Electricity Business
data.description:
Chevron (CVX) has multiple value drivers at play that may develop to generate stronger earnings over the coming years, including cost improvements, energy production, and enhanced oil recovery technologies. Though the macroeconomic environment for oil remains relatively uncertain with a supply/demand imbalance that may pressure prices in the near term, the demand outlook remains robust with modest growth expected over the coming years with an expected 1.38mmbbl/d in demand growth for 2026. Looking beyond near-term headwinds, I believe CVX shares may realize modest growth in the coming years driven by its operational improvement efforts. I am recommending CVX shares with a "Buy" rating with a price target of $183/share at 7.20x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Chevron Corporation Operations

The energy industry started the year off with a jolt of strength following the capture of Nicolas Maduro, President of Venezuela. In addition to this, the oil demand forecast remains robust for the next two years, though supply & demand are expected to remain at an imbalance, potentially pressuring prices in the near term.

The EIA is forecasting global oil production to increase by 1.4mmbbl/d while U.S. production declines by less than 1% for 2026 and further by 2% in 2027. The agency is expecting Brent crude to decline in price throughout the year to roughly $56/bbl, down from $69/bbl in 2025, a major headwind for the broader industry. As for natural gas, prices are expected to realize some weakness in 2026 before seeing price growth to an average of $4.60/mcf in 2027 due to growing demand for LNG exports and domestic gas power production.

OPEC released a more optimistic outlook with demand expected to increase by 1.38mmbbl/d in 2026 and 1.34mmbbl/d in 2027 while pausing production hikes in early 2026.

In the United States, Midland, Texas, is experiencing the challenges set forth by weaker oil prices followed by a sharp decline in operating rigs in the Permian Basin throughout 2025. Thematically, I believe there may be a modest shift towards more reliance on offshore production given the slower decline curve relative to onshore unconventional production.

Nonetheless, Chevron’s domestic production remained strong in q3’25, growing by 16% net of North Dakota production. Referencing Chevron’s 2025 Analyst Day, management is expecting growth to remain durable for oil in the coming years, requiring significant reinvestment to maintain supply. As for natural gas, management is expecting data center growth to drive demand for domestic gas, which I believe may be facilitated by associated gas produced in the major oil basins like in the Permian Basin and Bakken Shale. Looking to future growth, management is expecting to grow production at a 2%-3% CAGR through 2030.

One of the biggest value drivers in unconventional oil is Chevron’s use of advanced chemical treatments to enhance oil recovery. Accordingly, Chevron is expecting a 10% uplift in production with an elongated decline curve. Essentially, this allows the firm to produce more oil per well with less capital.

The most appealing asset Chevron owns is in the Stabroek Block offshore Guyana, which has a breakeven cost at around $35/bbl. Chevron acquired these assets through the acquisition of Hess Corp, which transferred a 30% equity interest in the assets. These assets are part of Chevron’s long-cycle production program, providing the firm with stability through oil cycles.

One major growth area Chevron has been working towards is in power generation, particularly for behind-the-meter electricity for large-scale AI data centers. Accordingly, Chevron is expecting to produce 2.5GW of gas-fired power in West Texas with plans to expand to 5GW with first power in 2027. Power production can tie together Chevron’s gas production in the Permian Basin, producing low-cost fuel for data center customers through long-term power purchase agreements [PPAs]. This can potentially be a beneficial market for Chevron to enter, creating a smooth baseline for cash flow generation through oil cycles. Though management voiced some hesitancy in expanding beyond 5GW, the growth opportunity for power demand for data centers could be a major serviceable business for Chevron, particularly in West Texas and potentially in northern states where data centers are being constructed. McKinsey is expecting power demand for large-scale data centers to drive power demand to 170-220GW in capacity. One of the niche factors that Chevron brings is its strategic positioning for gas production to be used in power generation, creating more certainty for power production stability.

As for operations in Venezuela, Chevron was quick to route 11 vessels following the capture of President Nicolas Maduro. Given the firm’s existing presence in the country, I believe Chevron may leverage its positioning to bolster its basin exposure as the United States aims to revitalize the country’s energy infrastructure. Accordingly, Chevron is expected to receive an expanded license for production to boost export capacity for heavy crude. Chevron currently has an estimated 240mbbl/d in production.

Venezuela could potentially be an appealing region to develop for Chevron given the roughly 303Bbbl oil reserves. My thoughts on the matter is that the United States could potentially displace Canadian crude imports with Venezuelan to bolster trade negotiations, a strategic move that may create a more favorable trade environment as part of USMCA. Though this is a speculative opinion, it wouldn’t be surprising if it were to be true given President Trump’s haste in creating a trade deal for oil between the US and Venezuela.

Chevron Corporation Financial Position

Management outlined an appealing growth outlook for Chevron for the coming years, particularly with the expectation to nearly double free cash flow per share over the next 5 years using 2025 as the base. Accordingly, the breakeven oil price to sustain Chevron’s capital deployment and dividend distribution is under $50/bbl Brent, while attaining its cash flow target is at $70/bbl Brent. Improved free cash flow generation is largely driven by more effective capital deployment, expecting to invest $18-$21 billion annually through 2030 to support both short- and long-cycled projects.

One of the mechanisms to drive free cash flow generation will be structural cost reductions in the range of $3-$4 billion by 2027, largely driven by asset divestitures, technological advancements, and 25% cost synergies resulting from the Hess acquisition.

Looking ahead to eq4’25, I’m forecasting Chevron to generate $49.5 billion in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $1.47/share. My rationale behind the forecast includes weaker oil prices driving down margins throughout the quarter. Nonetheless, I’m forecasting improved margins throughout the coming years as oil demand continues to increase. With OPEC+ holding off on releasing capacity, oil prices may remain relatively stagnant for the time being in the low $60/bbl Brent.

Risks Related to Chevron Corporation

Bull Case

Increased oil demand with production constraints by OPEC+ can potentially support improved oil prices in the medium term. Chevron’s transition towards power production can drive stability in cash flow streams and may be a potential growth market for the coming years. Chevron remains committed to returning cash to shareholders, providing a robust 4.12% dividend yield and significant share buybacks.

Bear Case

Oil prices may remain in the low-$60/bbl Brent despite continued production curtailment by OPEC+. Chevron’s ambitious goals for earnings and cash flow growth may be challenged if oil prices remain subdued.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

CVX shares are currently trading at a relatively rich premium of 8.61x EV/aEBITDA, just above its U.S. peer Exxon Mobil (XOM) at 8.04x. Despite the higher premium, Chevron’s distribution yield provides a more appealing investment outlay at 4.12%, nearly 1% above XOM shares’ dividend yield of 3.19%. A critical differentiator between Chevron and Exxon Mobil is the firms' differing approaches to the power business. Chevron is taking a direct approach as a power producer, whereas Exxon Mobil is aiming to provide carbon capture storage technology to the power producers.

Chevron remains committed to returning value to shareholders, targeting buybacks in the range of $10-$20 billion annually when oil is priced at $60-$80/bbl Brent. Along with the robust distribution, shareholder returns may remain strong for the coming years, providing incremental value over time for investors.

Given the financial outlook and macroeconomic environment, I believe CVX shares may realize modest growth in the coming years; I’m valuing shares at $183/share at 7.20x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA. I am recommending CVX shares with a "Buy" rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: NAHB Housing Market Index: Builder Confidence Moved Lower To Start 2026
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Builder confidence moved lower at the start of 2026, remaining in negative territory for the 21st consecutive month. The National Association of Home Builders (NAHB) Housing Market Index (HMI) dropped two points from December to 37 this month. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 40.

The National Association of Home Builders Housing Market Index is a gauge of builder opinion on the relative level of current and future single-family home sales.

The data is collected from a monthly survey of about 900 home builders asking respondents to, "rate market conditions for the sale of new homes at the present time and in the next six months as well as the traffic of prospective buyers of new homes." It is a diffusion index, which means that a reading above 50 indicates a favorable outlook on home sales; below 50 indicates a negative outlook.

Here are a few quotes from the press release:

“While the upper end of the housing market is holding steady, affordability conditions are taking a toll on the lower and mid-range sectors,” said NAHB Chairman Buddy Hughes, a home builder and developer from Lexington, N.C. “Buyers are concerned about high home prices and mortgage rates, with downpayments particularly challenging given elevated price to income ratios.”

“In a positive development, Freddie Mac reported that the average mortgage rate fell to 6.06% as of Jan. 15, the lowest rate in three years and nearly 100 basis points below the same period last year,” said NAHB Chief Economist Robert Dietz.

Here is the historical series, which dates from 1985.

Components of the NAHB Housing Market Index

The NAHB Housing Market Index is calculated based off of three components: current sales, expected sales in the next six months, and traffic of prospective buyers. All three components declined in January.

Housing Market Index: Regional Breakdown

The chart below gives a regional breakdown of the NAHB HMI in four regions of the country: Northeast, Midwest, South, and West.

Housing Market Index and Consumer Attitudes

The HMI correlates fairly closely with broad measures of consumer confidence. Here is a pair of overlays with the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index.

ETFs associated with home builders include Invesco Dynamic Building & Construction ETF (PKB), iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF (ITB) and SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: T-Mobile: 6% From The Bonds Is Not Enough
data.description:
T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS) is one of the leading telecommunications companies in the United States, with assets of over $217 billion and a solid investment-grade rating. Recently, we have been finding value in long-term baby bonds and want to examine what the instruments of TMUS offer to the public again.

TMUS Basic Information

  • Total Assets (Q3 2025): close to $217.2 billion
  • Current Market Capitalization: around $215 billion
  • Nominal Value of Exchange-Traded Debt: close to $1.19 billion
  • Performance:

The 5-year total return on NAV of the company is close to 9.82%, and the 10-year total return is around 17.6%:

  • News:

BELLEVUE, Wash.--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- T-Mobile US, Inc. (NASDAQ: TMUS) (“T-Mobile”) announced today that T-Mobile USA, Inc., its direct wholly-owned subsidiary (“T-Mobile USA” or the “Issuer”), has agreed to sell $1,150,000,000 aggregate principal amount of its 5.000% Senior Notes due 2036 (the “2036 Notes”) and $850,000,000 aggregate principal amount of its 5.850% Senior Notes due 2056 (the “2056 Notes,” and collectively with the 2036 Notes, the “notes”) in a registered public offering.

The offering of the notes is scheduled to close on January 12, 2026, subject to satisfaction of customary closing conditions. T-Mobile USA intends to use the net proceeds from the offering for refinancing existing indebtedness on an ongoing basis, or other general corporate purposes.

LLC, ING Financial Markets LLC, Mizuho Securities USA LLC, Morgan Stanley & Co. LLC, MUFG Securities Americas Inc., NatWest Markets Securities Inc., PNC Capital Markets LLC, Santander US Capital Markets LLC, Scotia Capital (USA) Inc., SG Americas Securities, LLC, SMBC Nikko Securities America, Inc., TD Securities (USA) LLC, Truist Securities, Inc., U.S. Bancorp Investments, Inc. and Wells Fargo Securities, LLC are the joint book-running managers for the offering of the notes. CIBC World Markets Corp. is acting as co-manager.

  • Credit Rating - "investment-grade":
  • Comparisons:

Below is shown a 5-year total return comparison between TMUS, the overall market, and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight Communication Services ETF(RSPC):

  • The common stock:

The common stock is currently traded at $192.02, and below you can see its price chart for the last year:

Baby Bonds

TMUS has three baby bonds: (TMUSI), (TMUSL), and (TMUSZ). All three are traded by the major credit agencies as an "investment-grade" (you can see their credit rating in the picture above).

In 2025, T-Mobile US, Inc. made an exchange offer for the old United States Cellular Corporation (USCC) bonds. Investors holding USCC bonds could exchange them for new T-Mobile USA, Inc. bonds with the same coupons and maturities: TMUSL, TMUSZ, and TMUSI. This transferred the debt from USCC to T-Mobile, while investors retained their bond yields and maturities. You can see more information about this event here. All three new bonds were issued on 08/05/2025. TMUSI and TMUSZ have a coupon rate of 5.50%, with close to 0.7% spread to the treasury yield, then, and currently it is close to 1.45%. TMUSL was issued with a coupon rate of 6.25%, with a spread to the treasury yield of 1.45%, and a current spread of 1.75%. All three bonds are high-duration and have close to 43-44 years to maturity.

At the time of writing, TMUSI and TMUSZ trade below par, and both have a yield to worst of around 6.25%, and TMUSL trades at par with a yield to worst of close to 6.45%.

We are facing a lot of questions about yield calculations, and we calculate them using the XIRR function in Google Sheets, which is supposed to be the most precise way when dealing with ex-dividend dates. It is a bit more work than just finding a random bond yield calculator, but at least we try to ensure the data is accurate.

And here you can see the different prices vs yields to worst charts of the TMUS's baby bonds:

T-Mobile US has strong financials and an investment-grade credit rating. The company continues to be active in the capital markets, providing opportunities for a variety of investors. As we are able to find many 7% investment-grade bonds, we rate the bonds of the company a solid "Hold".

data.title: Carrier Global: Unable To Carry Its Weight Amidst Continued Softness
data.description:
By the middle of October, I concluded that Carrier Global Corporation (CARR) was giving its investors the chills. Following a successful and full transformation to become an HVAC business, the company has seen headwinds amidst soft trends in North American residential volumes, driven largely by industry legislation changes.

While the near-term outlook is challenging, the medium- and long-term outlooks remain solid, and while some turbulence is seen in the near- to medium-term, improvements should come through hopefully at some point in 2026.

I have lost a bit of faith in this recovery following the commentary in the third quarter earnings report and anecdotal evidence that weakness will persist heading into 2026. This, in combination with relentless focus on buybacks (at elevated levels), makes me cautious to get involved here.

A Mega Transition

Carrier used to be somewhat of a mini conglomerate operating within United Technologies, until that business merged with RTX (RTX) in 2020, at which point Carrier became a standalone business.

At the time of the separation, Carrier generated about $19 billion in sales from HVAC, fire & safety products, as well as refrigeration products. The HVAC business was responsible for half of sales, with company-wide margins reported in the mid-teens.

Shares traded at levels in the mere teens, even as earnings power was seen around $2 per share, as investors were skeptical about the business and leverage taken on. Ever since, everything was about to change as the company divested its Chubb fire & security business in a $3 billion deal.

The company further sold the Global Access Solutions business in another $5 billion deal, all while German-based Viessmann Climate Solutions was bought in a $13 billion deal, amidst many other smaller acquisitions and divestments announced as well.

Following strong demand and appraisal for these moves, shares rose to $80 in the fall of 2024. In between the many moving items, Carrier was on track to generate over $22 billion in sales while posting operating margins in the mid-teens, with earnings power seen around $2.50 per share.

The company guided for 2025 earnings to advance to $2.75-$3.00 per share; that was for a business that was 85% focused on HVAC operations, as shares traded at 23-25 times forward earnings, all while some leverage was still apparent.

Stagnation Kicks In

Amidst the transition in the business, reported sales and margins have not advanced that much between 2020 and 2024, and with expectations getting quite manageable early in the year, shares were vulnerable to a setback. In between the many moving targets, the company guided for 2025 sales to come in rather flat (on a reported basis) in between $22.5 billion and $23.0 billion, with earnings seen around $3.00 per share.

That is precisely what has happened in 2025, as shares were down to the $56 mark in October, having lost about 30% of their value. This came even as organic growth came in at 2% in the first quarter of 2025 and as much as 6% in the second quarter, as the company actually hiked the full-year outlook. Sales were seen around $23.0 billion, with earnings seen around $3.05 per share. However, net debt of $9.6 billion was substantial by all accounts.

Trading at $56 per share, the 866 million shares of the business granted equity of Carrier a $48.5 billion valuation in October, for a $58 billion enterprise valuation. This was equal to over 2 times sales, around 13 times EBITDA, seen around $4.5 billion, with leverage ratios seen around 2 times.

With earnings multiples down to 18–19 times, quite some multiple compression has been seen, all while organic growth is reported and debt is substantial, but more than in check. The issue is that of real issues in the residential business, with third-quarter volumes down 40%, driven by soft consumer demand and inventory reductions, cutting sales by an estimated half a billion.

These headwinds were real, with the company cutting 2,000 jobs while trimming the earnings outlook to $2.80 per share, even as data center-related revenues doubling to a billion could not offset this weakness. While real headwinds were seen in the business, these were mostly short- to medium-term issues, as I was hoping for a re-rating in 2026.

Trading Flat

Since October, shares of Carrier have traded flat while trading in a $50-$60 range. By the end of October, Carrier reported third quarter results, with reported sales down 7% and organic sales down 4%, with falling sales driven by the aforementioned trends. Both reported, and adjusted margins took a roughly 250-300 basis points beating given the soft sales trends, with adjusted earnings down ten cents to $0.67 per share.

As a result, the company cut the full-year sales guidance by a billion to $22 billion, while cutting the adjusted earnings per share number to $2.65 per share, down forty cents from the revised guidance.

Buybacks made that the share count come down to 858 million shares, as net debt has risen to $10.5 billion, as the company continues to aggressively buy back shares. In between the lower earnings, earnings multiples have increased up to 21 times, as the setback is quite severe. After all, the implied fourth quarter sales guidance is seen around $5.1 billion, down from a $5.6 billion number in the third quarter.

In the aftermath of the earnings report, the company announced a near 7% increase in the quarterly dividend, now payable at $0.24 per share, payable at $0.96 per share each year, for a yield near 2%.

A smaller deal was announced in December, as Carrier announced an agreement to sell the Riello business to the Ariston Group. Gross proceeds from the sale are seen at $430 million, this being less than a percent of the enterprise valuation, yet no other financial details have been announced.

And Now?

Frankly, I am a bit more reserved than I was in October, with shares trading at similar levels. For starters, the third quarter was weak, with the full-year guidance cut being worse than I anticipated. Furthermore, the company continues to be aggressive with net buybacks, pushing up net debt to over $10 billion, even pro forma the recent deal, as management has been aggressive and early with buybacks.

Moreover, the business will see a rough start to 2025 from the residential business, as the data center component of the business is just 5%, too small to create a strong tailwind for the entire business. As management confirmed continued weakness on a recent conference call for the months of November and December, the company is certainly ending the year on a softer note.

In between continued softness heading into 2026, there are some benefits seen from restructuring efforts, a lower tax rate, and the incremental impact from a reduced share count. It is fair to say that it is too early to see any meaningful growth, if any, for 2026 earnings here.

In between a flattish share price performance and softer trends, I am getting more cautious. Given the softer end to the year. Continued softness was seen into 2026, as I lack conviction given the uneven news events in the final quarter of the year.

data.title: Jaguar Mining: Limited Margin Of Safety At Current Levels
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. MTPA = million tonnes per annum. FS/DFS = Definitive Feasibility Study.

Introduction

Jaguar Mining (JAGGF) is a single-asset ~40,000 ounce gold producer in Brazil for the time being, given that its MTL Complex (Turmalina Mine and plant) is in care and maintenance following a major slump in its Satinoco dry-stack tailings pile in December 2024. Fortunately, this incident did not result in any injuries or fatalities but led to damage to nearby homes, damage to on-site equipment and nearly 90 families moved to temporary accommodations.

On a positive note, Jaguar has since completed considerable remediation work with slope reconfiguration and stabilization of areas adjacent to its processing and paste fill plants and the completion of a new emergency escapeway at the MTL Mine. Additionally, the Satinoco tailings pile has been stabilized, removed from service and future tailings will be placed in the historic Faina Pit (next two years) and approvals are in place for a longer-term solution.

As for hopes for a restart, Jaguar Mining shared that the National Mining Agency ["ANM"] lifted restrictions on January 7th after a more than year-long shutdown, enabling execution of stages 3, 4 and 5 of its Gradual Resumption Plan. This will include the restart of UG development, the restart of UG mining and the restart of its processing plant. However, the resumption of operations is still contingent on final clearance from the Environmental Emergency Office ["NEA"] which is independent from ANM and exclusively responsible for environmental matters.

Hence, while much work has been done to move towards a restart, there is no firm date yet for its MTL Complex.

Further, while investors are closer to seeing Jaguar return to its status as a dual-asset ~70,000 ounce per annum gold producer, 2025 was a huge disappointment. This is because while most producers were reporting record cash flow, stacking cash on the balance sheet and buying back shares, in some instances, Jaguar diluted shareholders by nearly ~7%, saw annual gold sales slide 40% and was one of the few companies to report negative free cash flow in Q3'25. Below are just some of the full-year highlights:

  • Gold production fell 38% year-over-year to ~40,300 ounces with no contribution from MTL Complex
  • Discovered that certain royalties were paid to incorrect landowners for a full three years (June 2022 to June 2025) with a $4.0 million expense provision for amounts now due to the correct landowners
  • Production per share slid 43% year-over-year on the back of lower production and ~7% share dilution

Despite the further trend lower in per share metrics, industry-lagging financial results and lack of free cash flow generation, Jaguar's share soared over 220% in 2025, outperforming the Gold Juniors Index (GDXJ). This came on the back of a management shake-up and a renewed growth outlook, with the company hoping to more than double production from 2025 levels looking out to 2027, with further growth to 100,000+ ounces next decade.

That said, this is hardly growth; it is a mere production recovery, and it's hardly a significant feat to double production after seeing it collapse 56% in the preceding five years. Further, costs continue to rise sharply across the portfolio, impacted by a stronger Brazilian Real, with AISC surging to $2,063/oz in its most recently reported results. So, while some recovery in the share price was overdue after massive underperformance in 2021 through 2024, I don't see any material change in the investment thesis here.

In fact, Jaguar continues to be a small-scale and highly concentrated producer from a NAV/cash flow standpoint, relying on two assets (one currently) and a single country for all of its cash flow. And while some investors seem to think US$100.00/share is the right value for this stock, implying a multiple of ~60x 2026 operating cash flow estimates at spot levels, I would argue that Jaguar is fully valued today, with the right multiple for a company with this track record, small assets and risk profile being 4x operating cash flow.

Further, while it should be pretty straight-forward to sell doré bars and concentrate, pay bills and pay royalty dues for these miners, it's not always as simple as higher metals prices reflecting higher cash balances for every producer. In Jaguar Mining's case, and as noted above, they've been paying the wrong landowners certain royalties for a full three years, they could be out an additional ~$4.0 million and will likely need to double pay these royalties that should have gone to the correct landowners in the first place.

In this update we'll dig into its preliminary Q4 results, recent developments and why I continue to see Jaguar as an inferior way to get gold price exposure.

H2'25 Production & Sales

Jaguar Mining ("Jaguar Mining") released its Q4'25 production results this week, reporting quarterly production of ~9,400 ounces, a 36% decline from the year-ago period. This sharp decline in production followed a 41% decline in Q3'25 with Jaguar continuing to rely on only a single asset as its MTL Complex remains in care and maintenance. As for results at solely its Pilar Mine (Caete Complex), production slid over 10% year-over-year, impacted by lower throughput (~96,200 tonnes vs. ~101,000 tonnes) and lower grades (3.41 G/T of gold vs. 3.65 G/T of gold).

The result was that Jaguar put together its fifth consecutive year of declining production with an industry-leading 56% decline in gold production in the period, reporting 2025 production of ~40,300 ounces. Worse, these assets haven't hit guidance once in the five-year period, with one of the worst beat/miss track records sector-wide, sporting an average miss of over 14%. Obviously, the bar is set quite low heading into 2026, with guidance to be provided once the MTL Complex is back up and running consistently.

Still, I would be surprised to see production come in at upwards of 70,000 ounces this year, leaving Jaguar as a small-scale and high-cost producer, with small-scale, concentrated, non Tier-1 jurisdiction and above-average cost producers typically commanding low single-digit multiples.

Financial Results

As for Jaguar's financial results, we are still awaiting its Q4'25 financial results, but its Q3'25 results were nothing to write home about, relying solely on Pilar and losing a year of cash build potential during a period of record gold prices. For starters, revenue was down 12% year-over-year to ~$34 million, cash flow sunk 45% to ~$7.0 million and free cash flow came in at [-] ~$5 million. Fortunately, Jaguar still exited 2025 with ~$60 million in net cash, albeit helped by a ~$22 million equity raise at ~US$3.96/share, placing it in a decent position to pursue growth when combined with cash flow generation this year at what continue to be very strong gold prices.

On a positive note, Q1'26 is shaping up to be another record for gold prices, with Jaguar set to have a solid quarter from a free cash flow standpoint even if AISC continues to hover above $2,000/oz. That said, the company continues to benefit far less than its peers running at normal production levels, and even if the gold price averages $4,500/oz this year, which assumes no real price correction, I wouldn't expect Jaguar to generate much more than $75 million in free cash flow (free cash flow defined as all capital expenditures, not just sustaining) this year, leaving it trading at barely ~7x FY2026 free cash flow estimates at spot levels.

For comparison, there are million-ounce producers with diversified production profiles and far superior track records of per share growth trading at barely 5x FY2026 free cash flow estimates at spot levels or almost 30% cheaper than Jaguar.

Costs & Margins

Moving to costs and margins, the picture isn't any prettier. In fact, Q3'25 cash costs of $1,374/oz (+25% year-over-year) and AISC soared to $2,063/oz (+13% year-over-year), well above the estimated 2026 industry average of ~$1,700/oz and over $500/oz above industry average levels in Q3'25.

These elevated costs were despite modest sustaining capital of just over $4 million in the period, and the Brazilian Real has remained elevated since Q3'25, with higher royalties (gold price) and an elevated Brazilian Real likely to contribute to AISC remaining near $2,000/oz and nearly double the levels it sat at just five years ago (FY2020 AISC: $1,044/oz). Fortunately, while Jaguar's cost control has been anything but impressive, gold prices have surged, more than offsetting the increased costs, with Jaguar's ASC margins improving to $1,402/oz (40.5%) vs. $643/oz (26.0%) in the year-ago period, a 1,450 basis point improvement.

That said, this is hardly a company-specific attribute that would make Jaguar Mining stand out, with all gold producers flourishing in the current environment. Further, Jaguar has more to lose in a declining gold price environment given its relatively small-scale operations with above-average costs, making it more sensitive to softness in gold prices.

Recent Developments

As for recent developments, the most significant is that Jaguar has completed most of the steps to re-open its Turmalina Complex for operation, but it is still waiting for final approval from the NEA. As for timing, Jaguar noted it hopes to restart production in Q1'26, suggesting the potential for a ramp-up to steady state production by mid-2026 latest. Further, higher gold prices have also placed a re-start of Santa Isabel (Paciência Complex) on the table, giving Jaguar three mines vs. one to enter 2027, potentially.

For now, though, Jaguar remains a one-legged stool with high concentration to a single asset. Further, while Jaguar could be a tri-asset producer by 2027, it would still be the smallest tri-asset producer sector-wide that I'm aware of, producing barely 80,000 ounces. Hence, although this certainly helps from a cash flow diversification standpoint and improves its higher risk profile, it does little from a re-rating standpoint given that junior producers tend to trade at a fraction of their larger-scale 1.0+ million ounce gold producer peers.

Valuation

Based on ~88 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$6.15, Jaguar Mining trades at a market cap of ~$540 million and an enterprise value of ~$480 million. This leaves Jaguar Mining trading at an estimated ~7.7x FY2026 enterprise value to free cash flow estimates using base case metals price assumptions ($4,100/oz gold), a figure that makes it more expensive than several producers sector-wide with much larger production profiles, ownership of Tier-1 (400,000+ ounces) and Tier-2 (250,000+ ounce) scale assets and better track records.

And while a high single-digit free cash flow multiple might appear cheap, I would argue that this is actually an expensive valuation for a Tier-2 jurisdiction producer at sub 100,000 ounce per annum scale with its track record. In fact, there are companies 10x the scale like Torex Gold (TORXF) trading at barely 6.5x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates with a better track record, lower operating costs and a 10+ million ounce resource inventory. Elsewhere, Alkane Resources (ALKEF) has over 3x the scale, Tier-1 only operations (Australia, Sweden), a stronger pipeline with a massive copper-gold project and trades at barely 5.5x 2026 EV/FCF estimates.

Hence, while I wouldn't call Jaguar Mining expensive today, I think it's a stretch to call 8x free cash flow cheap for a small-scale producer in Brazil with small assets and a history of per share destruction.

So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 4.0x FY2026 cash flow estimates (35% weighting) and 1.0x P/NAV (9% discount rate, 65% weighting) and base case metals price assumptions ($4,100/oz gold in 2026), Jaguar's updated fair value comes in at US$6.50, pointing to less than 8% upside from current levels. At less conservative metals price assumptions of $4,500/oz this year, Jaguar Mining's fair value jumps to US$8.10.

That said, I am looking for a minimum 40% discount to fair value for small-scale Tier-2 or Tier-3 jurisdiction producers and ideally closer to 45% to justify entering new positions and ensuring an adequate margin of safety. Even using the upper end of this range, Jaguar Mining would need to dip to US$3.90 or lower to become more interesting from a valuation standpoint, suggesting there's little way to justify paying up for the stock here.

Obviously, my view of fair value may different from the market, and in the ebullient period we've found ourselves in, a rising tide will lift all boats. Still, I think it makes sense to focus on higher-quality companies with above-average quality assets when they are hated and trading at a deep discount to fair value. Today, Jaguar is neither high-quality, hated, nor trading at a deep discount to fair value, making the stock an avoid at US$6.15, with far more attractive opportunities elsewhere in the sector.

Technical Picture

Finally, looking at the technical picture, Jaguar Mining remains in the upper portion of its support/resistance range and is now getting more vulnerable to a sizeable correction. Hence, with a less attractive technical picture and no margin of safety from current levels, I don't see any way to justify paying up for the stock here.

Summary

Jaguar Mining had another disappointing year in 2025 with sharply declining production, a material increase in costs and limited free cash flow generation, with this being even more disappointing given that the gold price tailwind allowed most producers to build significant cash on their balance sheet. Worse, Jaguar is still operating as a one-legged stool with its second mining complex (Turmalina) offline, with no clear restart date yet. The result is that it's seeing half of the benefit of record gold prices that its peers are enjoying.

Assuming the stock were trading at substantially lower levels and a low single-digit free cash flow multiple using more conservative gold pricing, there might be a trade setup here. However, at an estimated ~7.7x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates ($4,100/oz gold) and ~5.6x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates ($4,500/oz gold), there are simply several higher-quality and lower-risk options available elsewhere at more attractive prices.

In summary, I continue to see Jaguar Mining as an Avoid, an inferior way to play the sector and I would view any rallies above US$6.55 before quarter-end as an opportunity to book profits into strength.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AerSale Stock: MRO Is The Change It Desperately Needs
data.description:
AerSale Corporation (ASLE) was once a stock many investors invested in because of the prospects of their AerAware product, providing an enhanced vision system. However, commercial success has remained absent, and the company has pivoted towards MRO (Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul) opportunities. I covered AerSale in October and the stock has climbed 3.8% since, which was higher than the S&P 500’s 1.2% return but still not great. In this report, I discuss the company’s post-earnings drop and update my price target.

AerSale's Stock Price Drop And The Positives

AerSale stock dropped after providing its Q3 2025 earnings, and that is likely also why the stock performance has not been substantially better. In my prior report, I already noted that AerSale’s results can be lumpy due to lumpiness of whole asset sales introducing a quarter-to-quarter variance, and that is exactly what hit the company in the third quarter. Revenue missed estimates by $22.66 million, and earnings per share of $0.00 missed estimates by $0.14. Looking at what caused the revenue miss, we note that nearly all the miss can be attributed to an absence of whole asset sales. In Q3 2025, the company sold no airplanes or engines, while it sold five engines in Q3 2024 for $22.6 million. We do note that the absence of asset sales is not necessarily a bad thing as each engine and airplane goes to a life cycle valuation assessment and based on that AerSale decides to use the asset for parts, sell the asset, or lease it. Particularly for the engines, we note that the company elected to use feedstock to drive used serviceable material sales and increase lease revenues.

It seems that the market has been focused on that miss and has not been able to appreciate the recurring business. The company saw higher lease revenue and used serviceable materials' activity. EBITDA during the quarter grew 16% despite missing engine sales, while EBITDA grew from 10% to 13.3% and gross margins improved from 28.6% to 30.2%. So, we see that the recurring business is performing rather well.

With AerAware not delivering on expectations. AerSale has pivoted to MRO activities with new MRO capacity expected to add $50 million in revenues, of which $25 million is expected in 2026. With new safety requirements coming into effect requiring products such as AerSale’s AerSafe solution and high MRO demand, the growth prospects are strong. The company also has a Boeing 757 freighter program. Market conditions have not been optimal for some time, but we note that there has been improvement and the company only needs to place the remaining three freighters.

Tone Change On AerAware

For a very long time, AerAware was presented as the product that customers could not wait to get their hands on, and the only thing holding up commercial success was the final certification activities that had to be carried out. However, post-certification we saw no major announcement. In my prior report, I detailed that from a commercial perspective, AerAware did not really appeal. It is basically a product that will not earn itself back of the service time of an airplane. What AerSale basically did was creating a product for which I believe commercial demand is non-existent unless there is some policy change that requires it, and we see that the company is now also framing it as such as shared during the Q3 2025 earnings call:

We believe the ongoing enhancements to the product, combined with the increased focus by both operators and the FAA on situational awareness, will drive long-term adoption of this advanced technology across the industry.

Key is the operation and FAA focus on situational awareness or putting it differently on safety. Furthermore, we note that there is no talk about near-term traction but about a long-term adoption. The company also tied AerAware to alleviate air traffic congestion. That in my view is not quite justified, as AerAware does extremely little to alleviate air traffic congestion. It is not the case that if AerAware is being installed on airplanes that we will see congestion magically improving. The use case for AerAware remains limited and as a result, the reduction in pressure on air traffic controllers is also limited.

AerSales seems to be positioning the product now as a safety tool and system to reduce congestion. I can agree on the first point, less so on the second point. There needs to be a policy change before AerAware will gain any traction, and that in my view shows the limited commercial appeal of the product.

AerSale Is An Underperformer Compared To Peers

While AerSale did outperform the S&P 500, it did substantially underperform the peer group. The performance shows more or less the same shape, but we note that AerSale lacks exposure to the defense markets and exposure to higher airplane build rates. So, apart from the growing MRO footprint, the company does not have the growth drivers that are often lifting the stock prices of aerospace and defense peers. Instead, it has an outsized exposure of non-recurring items on its financial results.

AerSale Stock Is An Unpredictable Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For AerSale, I am adjusting the price target from $14.94 to $12.72 driven by a reduction in the expected EBITDA for 2025. Driven by the variability in the business, which includes timing of whole asset sales and acquisition of feed stock to drive the used serviceable material business, I am using 2025 expected earnings to value the stock rather than the 2026 expected earnings. Furthermore, I am assuming that the company converts around 60%-65% of its EBITDA to free cash flow.

As the company is increasingly focused on MRO, we see that EBITDA expectations are increasing, but also note that new capacity going online has to grow to a learning curve. For that reason, 2026 margins are not expected to see a major hike, as the company expects half of the incremental capacity being added to be utilized in terms of revenues in 2026. While the company definitely sees variability in revenues and cash flow, my current expectation is that net debt will decrease to near-zero over the next two years.

Conclusion: AerSale Is A Higher Risk Buy

AerSale has not been able to successfully commercialize AerAware, and we now also see a change in tone regarding the commercialization path and the value proposition of the product. I believe that AerAware is just a good engineering product, but without any strong market appeal. Any order that the company can get for AerAware will be considered a win, but I do not believe that this will be the broad success that the company had initially hoped for, and it is increasingly relying on safety policies making a system such as AerAware mandatory. The real growth driver for AerSale is going to be MRO. There is a strong margin and revenue opportunity there that should make the company’s results more predictable. I am maintaining my buy rating, but do note that until the company ramps up MRO and parts sales, it will still be exposed significantly to whole asset sales. Sometimes those sales come in strong, sometimes they come in extremely weak.

data.title: Invitation Homes And American Homes 4 Rent Just Got Interesting
data.description:
Single-Family Rental (SFR) REITs dropped materially on news that Trump is considering banning large institutional investors from buying single-family homes. It is the latest leg down (drop on right side of chart) in what has already been a down year.

For years we have not had much interest in the single-family rental REITs because they traded at premium valuations, and we considered them overvalued. However, with the latest drop, they have finally entered value territory.

With newly interesting market prices, we will be adding Invitation Homes (INVH) and American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) to our active coverage.

Let us begin with a discussion of industry level fundamentals and follow with the valuation and outlook of the individual REITs.

A successful sector facing a current headwind

The SFR REITs have performed very well on a fundamental level, each growing AFFO/share materially and consistently.

A key contributor to their success has been their ability to leverage scale to improve both margins and customer satisfaction.

Single-Family Rental, aside from the big REITs, is highly fragmented, consisting primarily of small businesses and individuals that own a handful of homes available for rent.

With a small number of homes, it is difficult for these smaller entities to have sufficient in-house expertise to deal with the variety of home repairs that are required over time. Some of the work has to be contracted out which hurts margins, and it is then done whenever the outside source has the time which hurts customer satisfaction.

The SFR REITs have a different level of scale. INVH and AMH own over 85,000 and 61,000 homes, respectively. Each services thousands more homes through JV ownership and/or 3rd party management. They concentrate their holdings such that they will have a few thousand homes in individual submarkets.

With that level of scale, it becomes efficient to have highly experienced property managers and mechanics in close proximity to the homes. This allows them to service customer requests faster and more cost effectively. The proximity also means they need fewer personnel per home.

AMH and INVH achieved 3Q25 rental operating margins of 55% and 56%, respectively.

Historically, the sector has achieved strong rental rate growth and consistently high occupancy. When the cost of houses shot up circa 2021, renting became more economical relative to owning.

High home prices were exacerbated by mortgage rates shooting up to over 7% which blockaded an increasing portion of the population out of home ownership. It proved to be a boon for rental demand.

This allowed both apartments and SFR REITs to increase rent significantly year after year while maintaining high occupancy.

In response to the prosperous economics of rentals, developers flooded the market with supply. Development of SFR boomed in 2022 with an extraordinary volume of deliveries starting in 2023.

While the development wave has subsided with regard to new starts, those already in the pipeline are still being delivered at an above average pace.

This wave of supply meant incumbent properties had to compete with new builds that often offer lease-up incentives. It has been a headwind for rental rates across the entire sector. Submarkets with more new supply felt greater pressure on rents with many submarkets dipping to slightly negative rent growth in recent periods.

Yardi Matrix breaks down the rent and occupancy changes by submarket:

The supply wave took a bite out of same-store NOI growth.

Invitation Homes’ same-store NOI growth dropped to about 1% from its former average of about 4%. This is largely due to them being concentrated in Phoenix, Atlanta and Florida markets, which each received a huge wave of supply.

American Homes 4 Rent was less impacted, largely keeping its ~4% organic same-store NOI growth rate.

AMH is a bit more spread out and owns properties in submarkets that were less supply heavy.

In the longer run it is unclear to me which property markets are better. While markets like Phoenix and Atlanta are hurting from the supply wave, they are excellent demand markets with strong job and population growth so I suspect they will bounce back nicely as supply subsides.

That is our overview of the SFR industry. It is high margin and has generally had strong organic growth but it is currently facing a headwind from the supply wave of 2022-2023 with the tail end of properties constructed at that time still being delivered.

As forward supply looks much lower while demand remains healthy, I suspect SFR will return to a healthy organic growth rate after a lull in 2025 and early 2026. There is still significant room for higher rental rates due to the abnormally large spread in affordability between renting and owning.

It is a sector with generally strong fundamentals which is why it has typically traded at high AFFO multiples. With the downtrend in pricing and the recent drop on the talk of banning institutional buying of homes, the AFFO multiples have gotten much cheaper.

Valuation

Below we charted AFFO in orange bars against market price. Note how AMH price to AFFO peaked in late 2021. Since 2021, however, market price has dropped materially while AFFO has continued to rise.

INVH has the same pattern except to an even more dramatic degree.

With higher AFFO and lower market price, they are now trading at the following multiples:

  • INVH – 16.0X AFFO
  • AMH – 17.8X AFFO

That puts them in-line with the REIT index, which is unusual for a sector that has consistently traded at a premium.

We at 2nd Market Capital like to look at valuation from a variety of angles. Using data from the 10-Ks of each company we estimated their enterprise values per square foot and per home.

INVH and AMH are trading at $288K and $302K per home, respectively. INVH and AMH have average home sizes of 1,880 and 1,990 square feet.

Most people have looked at Zillow and other home buying sites so this is a metric that can easily be compared to real-world pricing. The EV/home looks to be slightly below market value of the houses.

Note that this is only looking at the wholly owned portion of their portfolios. INVH and AMH also have partial interest in another 7,622 and 3,376 houses. INVH also does 3rd party management for another 17,6278 homes.

Overall, both companies are trading at sizable discounts to net asset value:

  • INVH- 68.7% of NAV
  • AMH – 75.7% of NAV

Fundamental Outlook

Near term AFFO growth looks a bit subdued, largely as a result of the supply wave. Forward consensus estimates for each company are shown below.

I think the market in pricing these companies down has over-indexed on the near term.

Slow AFFO/share growth during a heavy headwind looks quite healthy to me. These are good companies that operate well and have consistently grown on a per share basis.

In my opinion, they are trading below fundamental value and are opportunistic. INVH would be my pick in the sector as it is both trading at a cheaper multiple and the larger of the 2 companies. I think it is being overly punished for weakness in Phoenix and Atlanta. While these submarkets are temporarily weak due to supply, they are excellent markets longer-term.

There are a lot of strong opportunities in REITs right now with the sector being broadly cheap, so we are not investing heavily in SFR as there are just too many things we want to buy. That said, I think it would be entirely reasonable to buy INVH at current valuation. It is a good growth company trading at a reasonable price.

Risk and opportunity related to a potential ban of institutional buying of homes

The current administration’s talk about banning institutional buying of homes is in its early stages. Until more details are released it is quite difficult to estimate the chance of it happening or the impact.

However, markets are forward looking, and it behooves us as investors to consider the possibilities.

So far, the market has priced it in as unequivocally negative. Prima facie, it sounds like a negative to have a company banned from buying its primary asset class.

However, anything that messes with supply can be quite beneficial to properties already in place.

As seen by the lower same-store NOI during the current supply wave, new supply is one of the largest risks to real estate. If institutions are banned in some way from buying SFR, the existing properties of INVH and AMH would not have to compete with as much new supply.

There would likely be less volume produced and what did filter into the SFR market would largely just be mom-and-pop operators which cannot effectively compete on margins or customer satisfaction.

Thus, a ban of some sort could have negative implications for external growth but also positive implications for organic growth. There is not yet enough information to estimate which impact would be larger. It is also unclear at this point if anything will actually happen with regard to the proposed ban.

Key pieces of information to watch for:

  • How all-inclusive would the ban be?
  • Can institutions still buy homes directly from homebuilders?
  • Can institutions buy homes out of foreclosure?
  • Can institutions buy homes on the secondary market?

Each outcome has different implications so I will be watching news on this matter as well as the market prices of the SFR REITs. My hunch is that the market’s initial reaction to news may not factor in the subtle implications to supply/demand.

data.title: ImmunityBio: Making Strides, But Beware The Hype (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

Market and analyst sentiment on ImmunityBio, Inc. (IBRX) has waxed and waned since I have been watching the company starting back in late 2023, prior to the approval of their first agent in bladder cancer. We're now finally getting clearer pictures of the market potential of this agent, as well as a wave of news related to their developmental pipeline, which now includes cellular therapies trying to build on engineered programs that we've had for a few years.

Today, let's focus on the results that have driven the surge in market value of IBRX stock from a low of around $2 to well over $5 per share at the time of writing, as well as why I think this rally is likely running a bit hot for now.

Pipeline Updates

Anktiva

The primary project continuing to be developed by IBRX is Anktiva, a "superagonist" of interleukin-15 designed to stimulate the production and activity of different immune cell populations in the context of cancer treatment. It is approved for the treatment of non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC) that has stopped responding to BCG, a weakened form of tuberculosis that is the current frontline standard of care after surgery in patients with early bladder cancer. Of note, this approval covers carcinoma in situ, with or without papillary histology, providing some important guardrails on the approved label at this time in the United States.

The biggest story for IBRX in 2025 related to challenges to get the label expanded to include patients who do not have carcinoma in situ, with the FDA issuing a refusal to file notice for the supplemental application. IBRX refuted that notice, stating that the FDA had provided "unambiguous guidance" earlier in the year that led them to submit the application. This disagreement has yet to be resolved, although IBRX has announced that the Saudi FDA has approved Anktiva for the wider label within their jurisdiction. Anktiva is also approved in Europe for carcinoma in situ with or without papillary histology.

For 2026, the next major clinical catalyst being showcased by IBRX is a potential readout of QUILT-2.005, evaluating Anktiva in patients with BCG-naive NMIBC, as enrollment has exceeded the expected pace and is on track to be complete by the next quarter. IBRX expects to use the findings from this study (primary endpoints being complete response rate and duration of response). We've previously seen results from the phase 1 portion of this trial, suggesting that adding Anktiva to BCG could improve the complete response rate at any time by 24% (85% vs. 61% for the experimental and BCG-only arms, respectively).

Those findings were encouraging, although limited in scope since the study only included 43 patients. However, the phase 1 readout gives a very interesting signal about the phase 2 trial, and I would view the readout later this year with optimism.

Circling back on ex-U.S. developments, the Saudi FDA was the first to approve Anktiva in the setting of non-small cell lung cancer. This was based on findings from a pair of studies in the metastatic setting, QUILT-3.055 and QUILT-2.023, which I covered in some detail in my last article.

Couple that with a recently announced readout showing strong recovery of lymphocytes, and the NSCLC continues to look promising to me. These developments in lung cancer were, in fact, the impetus for shifting my sentiment from relatively negative to tentatively positive at that time. A confirmatory phase 3 trial combining Anktiva with tislelizumab (an anti-PD-1 antibody) and docetaxel in previously treated NSCLC is ongoing.

Cell Therapy Programs

Anktiva also plays into the nascent pipeline of cell therapies being developed by IBRX. These include engineered NK cells targeting CD19 or PD-L1, as memory cytokine-enriched NK cells (M-ceNK cells). The CD19 program was the subject of the most recent news release on this front. IBRX announced that CD19-directed NK cells could induce durable remission in the setting of Waldenström macroglobulinemia, a form of non-Hodgkin lymphoma.

The results were taken from patients enrolled in the ongoing QUILT-106 study, which continues to enroll patients to receive treatment with these NK cells, and a follow-up study is planned to combine Anktiva with these cells.

Financial Overview

At the end of Q3 2025, IBRX held $60.2 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $197.6 million in marketable securities. Notable debts include $500.8 million in related-part convertible notes, as well as $316.1 million in revenue interest.

Product revenue was $31.8 million for the quarter, up from $5.9 million in Q3 2024. In a recent announcement, IBRX guided a preliminary net product revenue for 2025 of $113 million.

Operating expenses reached $87.7 million, comparable to the year prior, and after interest and other accounting items, the net loss for the quarter was $67.3 million. For the first 9 months of 2025, IBRX used $234.6 million in cash for operating activities. This implies a cash runway of approximately 3 to 4 quarters, assuming no other interventions.

It is worth noting that IBRX conducted various equity raises in 2025, bringing in almost $350 million. They have continued to prove resilient in terms of financing through Nant Capital, a private investment vehicle owned by the company's CMO.

Strengths and Risks

Strength - Strong chance to read out a definitive study in 2026

IBRX has a real shot to pursue its second major approval within the next few years. The thesis that they can achieve a dramatic revenue ramp is being unfurled before our eyes, and expanding labels and carving out new niches remain the key strategies that might push this to a long-term win. IBRX looks to be successfully executing both on the front of exploiting the current NMIBC label to its fullest extent in conjunction with finding where else the drug fits.

And if they're able to succeed on any of their cell therapy fronts, that would further solidify the growing juggernaut that the market is currently anticipating.

Risk - Debt load remains high

With a debt load sitting above $800 million, IBRX stands alone among the early commercial biotechs in terms of their outlays. This would appear to be an insurmountable climb, but it is worth keeping in mind that the debt is mostly held by entities controlled by the CMO Patrick Soon-Shiong. Still, servicing this debt is a drag on the road to profitability in the long term, and it is something that you should keep your eyes wide open on if considering an investment.

Risk - Despite sales growth, profitability remains far on the horizon

All talk aside about the growth of sales and the company's recent showcase of the eye-popping "700% growth in net product revenue," it is worth keeping in perspective that this growth is due mainly to being in the first full year of sales. And those net revenues of approximately $113 million are nothing to sneeze at, but they pale in comparison to other companies with one successful drug, such as Exelixis. And they pale in comparison with operating expenses, covering just over 1 quarter of the company's current outlay. The latter company has succeeded in building a major franchise brick by brick, approval by approval, not only by fully exploiting their first main opportunity.

It is important to keep in perspective the fact that setbacks like those we saw with the FDA in 2025 are not going to be one-off challenges. Bad news can and will visit IBRX as they continue to develop Anktiva further, and their cell therapy plays are very much in the proving stages and cannot yet be counted on to drive the pipeline further.

Bottom Line Summary

IBRX continues to have the legs of a stock with a large, devoted following, which has provided longevity far exceeding what I would view as a reasonable expectation. The rub is that, unlike my first negative sentiments of this stock, IBRX is now executing, and we're seeing signs that they have what it takes to make Anktiva a really successful product. When will that turn the company into a profitable venture? That's anyone's best guess, and a lot of people are betting right now that it's closer than you think.

If you're in that camp, it's hard to argue right now that there is much reason for pessimism with respect to IBRX. They have generated a lot of good news, new inroads, new approvals (outside the United States), and remain poised to announce potentially groundbreaking results in new indications this year.

Does that justify the current 46 price-to-sales ratio for the company, assuming the $113 million figure cited by the company is accurate? Time will tell, and IBRX likely has shown an ability to fund their ventures throughout. But for the near term, I think this hype is likely to deflate somewhat, and while the long-term story for IBRX remains optimistic (assuming they continue to execute), I expect we'll see a more favorable entry point before reaching those lofty goals being predicted by devoted stockholders.

As such, after substantial gains from my last Buy sentiment, I am revising to a Hold for now, given the uncertainty in terms of execution and what I suspect is a strong likelihood for a correction as market attention and enthusiasm wane.

data.title: Neste: 2025's Tremendous Returns Are The Start Of Recovery
data.description:
It's not fair or accurate to say that Neste has recovered or beaten my long-term estimates since I started writing about the company. Neste (NTOIF) has still been a very volatile company with plenty of ups and downs that, for many investors, including me, make it dependent on the positioning and timing, whether you're profitable or not. But I did buy shares and expand my position in 2025. And the return for doing that, if we look at the company's return for today, is massive. Massive is not a word I use lightly - I reserve it for triple-digit returns and similar "beats" that we see rarely.

We see it now, and we see it with Neste.

For the full story on Neste, I'm obviously recommending that you check out the entirety of my article series on the company, which is about 2-3 years old at the time of writing this article. I believe that the takeaway for this article, however, should be that undervalued stocks tend to recover at some point - and patience is key. Patience and not investing in companies that are arguably overvalued. Unfortunately, one of the things I did initially when I started investing in Neste was go in a bit "too early". It's something that is a tendency of mine, both on the "going in" and on the "going out".

One of the main questions for this article is whether I'll be selling my stake, divesting, and taking profits. For the time being, I don't believe that I will. The danger of the overvalued market is there, but I believe that Neste's (comparatively small, by the way) recovery marks not a peak in valuation, but merely the start to a recovery trend.

Of course, if you're "tired" of Nestes volatility and would rather have cash on hand, that's something I'd understand and applaud (and you should consider selling at that time).

So let's see what we have here.

Neste - the bottom was in, my estimate was correct.

In my previous articles, I've made it very clear that the primary correlation for Neste's results always was, and will continue to keep being, the margins and trends for its renewable fuels. During my early and later coverage, these have been negative - but as of the latest results, they have significantly improved. It's not wrong to call the turnaround a dramatic upswing in margin and pricing for renewable diesel. Of course, the reasons for outperformance was more than just that. The company's management has also implemented a turnaround plan, and while this is going to take time to see full effects, the results thus far have been encouraging.

My primary goal in this article is going to be to show you why I don't believe the current surge in valuation is more than the beginning for Neste, why we're bound to see much more upside than this.

First off, I want to point out how much, historically speaking, Neste has been able to outperform. The degree of "beating" that Neste has done over time is significant, because on a 2-year basis with a 20% margin of error, Neste has beaten estimates by more than that, 72%+ of the time. It's the highest "beating frequency" for a company this size thati have ever covered. Let me remind you at this time that Neste is neither small nor insignificant, but has a market capitalization of nearly €15.6B at the time of writing this article. It's also the largest refiner in all of Finland. So this forecast accuracy I am showing you here is not irrelevant.

The second argument or determination about upside and why more upside is more of a valuation-oriented and earnings-oriented one. In the case of either forecasted results or better than forecasted results, as I've said before and shown you above, the likelihood of outperformance here is significant. The company's results for 2025-2026E are likely to be between €0.5-€1/share. Even if the company doesn't beat those forecasts, the following years are currently showing even higher, with 2028E expectations putting it close to €2/share for that fiscal.

If that turns out to be accurate, even the current share price of over €20/share for the native is likely to be cheap for what Neste could offer.

Recent results confirm the company's overall upside. The current estimate is to deliver well in excess of the earlier estimated €300M EBITDA improvement by the end of 2026, improving fixed and variable costs while at the same time executing on profitable investments. These profitable investments include the Rotterdam capacity expansion, set to provide the company with yet more growth vectors.

Operational achievements should be the focus here; however, the quarterly results justify it. The company's SAF volumes, one of the more important indicators, were at record high levels, meaning that the company is leveraging the market that's currently offering very strong dynamics and potential for the business.

It's basically fair to say that the scenario that I have been forecasting on the positive side for the company has materialized, to some extent. It's not yet there in full - not entirely. That's where the upside for 2028E comes in.

The best explanation not only for Neste's long underperformance, but also for its recent upswing, is best done with an image/graphic. Remember how I said that renewable/SAF margins are the primary correlation - here is how that has looked, and how things look now.

As you can see, recovery is in full force here. Even the marketing & service segment saw improved trends and results, though this was as much due to seasonality as anything else. The company's investment activity has also gone down significantly, which is contributing to the upside and to the positive cash flow. Remember, though, that due to the company's specifics, cash flow will remain volatile here.

More importantly, perhaps, Neste has not significantly increased or exceeded its leverage or targets. For about 3 quarters now, the level has been at or close to 38%, which is below the target of 40%.

Regulatory news is neither small nor insignificant this latest period. The RED III directive, meaning it's a piece of legislation that EU members have to implement nationally over time, is going through transposition, including Central Europe. The ReFuelEU Aviation mandate is now at 2%, with further economic incentives in discussion. Imagine, for a second, an EU-wide implementation or requirement of environmental fuel for all aircraft fuelling or flying inside EU airspace. This is what we're talking about, and what I have been seeing in terms of upside for Neste for some time.

On the NA side of things, things are looking more mixed, which I believe is to be believed based on the current political administration.

On the one hand, the RVO, or renewable obligations, have increased for 2026-2027, but support only domestic feedstock and production. The LCFS amendments in California went into effect mid last year, which resulted in more balanced credit conditions, but most of the state ventures and programs with economic incentives support only domestic production. Because Neste's domestic NA production is comparatively low, it will make the EU market more important going forward, the NA market less so.

Generally speaking, I believe a continued bullish view on Neste, which I have, is dependent on continued high margins for renewable fuel and success in the company's investments and capacity expansions.

I believe the case for this is good, and therefore I am bullish.

Valuation for Neste - the risk is higher now, but the upside is still decent/high as well.

Neste has realized a lot of its upside that is implied in 2025 and 2026. What I mean by that is that if the company was to trade and generate as forecasted in the coming year, the current share price of €20 wouldn't be all that cheap in general - the upside instead comes from what happens in the years of 2027E and 2028E.

This is the main reason why this article represents a bit of a "risk increase" for Neste, and while taking profits its certainly not something I am doing just yet, is something that in certain situations could be entirely understandable at this price. The upside to a 20-year P/E average, which I believe to be a good forecast basis, is about 20% per year.

While this is certainly solid, it's not really anything earth-shattering. There are plenty of undervalued businesses in the market with that sort of upside, even with a better yield, and certainly less volatile than Neste. In order for you to invest here, you should believe in the fundamental appeal of renewable fuels and their expansion, because if that is the case, then this is potentially only the beginning for Neste. The suggested outperformance would also imply that there's much more upside here than analysts, investors, and the market may expect.

The obvious problem with such an assumption is the risk inherent in investing in it. How do we adjust or discount for this risk? I adjust for it by discounting the company's price to market. Instead of 18-19x P/E, I forecast, as I've mentioned before, at 15x. This implies a €28-€30/share fair value at most, which at this time would necessitate a PT increase to about €22.5/share, but not higher than this. That also represents my new price target and fair value range for the company, with the understanding that it might go higher (but also might go lower).

The energy sector is a tricky one. I'm more on the fence about it than I was 6 months ago, and am exiting some investments that I believe have reached their potential. Neste is, at least not just yet, not one of these investments, however.

For that reason, the risks and my thesis for the company are as follows

Risks to my thesis

Unfortunately, risks remain relatively prominent here, and are now supplemented with at least some valuation-related risks after more than a 100% RoR.

Neste is a play that's almost entirely dependent on volatile end-market refining and sales margins, which have proven themselves to be volatile in the past, and may do so again. While the recent moves, which are likely to make things more attractive here with minimum demands for SAF from the EU, are likely to somewhat improve things, a true recovery for Neste requires a recovery in end-market margins (which has happened as of 3Q, and is likely to continue, adding to this generally positive outlook)

Further risks include potential entry from established oil majors who are already committed to entering renewable markets, including BP (BP) and Chevron (CVX). The US domestic support could further add to this risk. I believe also that risks include continued trade policies, export trends, and the oil market and energy market overall.

I, however, believe that the risks are worth it—and at aa 22.5% upgrade to €22.5 PT on a fair value of €26-€30 for 2027-2028, I believe the upside is still worth it, although not nearly as much as previously. It makes my case for the company as an investment at lower multiples than now.

My updated PT for 2026 for the ADR NTOIF, which is a SHARE ADR, comes to a $26.14 price target, which I believe is good enough for investing.

My thesis for 2026 is now as follows.

Thesis

Neste is perhaps one of the most interesting oil/energy companies in Europe. They've found their niche, and they've pivoted at what I view as exactly the right time (proven by a significant return of over 100% since my last article) to serve a market that's going to need their products for the next few decades at the very least - and this continues to be my stance for 2026-2028E.

The recent surge and turnaround, I believe, coupled with the underlying trends in the energy industry and institutional support for renewable fuels, continues to make a case for investing in the company.

At a current forward valuation of under 15x, based on an estimated fair value above €26/share and a PT of €22.5/share updated for the 2026E period as of January, I consider Neste to be a "BUY". It's not a speculative one, but it's not as stable as some. The company is cheap, under €10/share, which is the cost basis for about 45% of my position in the company I hold right now.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of €22.5/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a “Buy rating as of January 2026.
